blocks.text: When it is present, people imitate it, and they long for it when it has gone; throughout all time it marches, crowned in triumph, victor in the contest for prizes that are undefiled.
But the prolific brood of the ungodly will be of no use, and none of their illegitimate seedlings will strike a deep root or take a firm hold.
For even if they put forth boughs for a while, standing insecurely they will be shaken by the wind, and by the violence of the winds they will be uprooted.
The branches will be broken off before they come to maturity, and their fruit will be useless, not ripe enough to eat, and good for nothing.
For children born of unlawful unions are witnesses of evil against their parents when God examines them.

3.13b–4.6: The second diptych: the moral strength of the virtuous. This central diptych counters the argument of the ungodly, who declared their might to be their law of right and what is weak to be useless (2.10–11).
But the righteous, though they die early, will be at rest.
For old age is not honored for length of time, or measured by number of years;
but understanding is gray hair for anyone, and a blameless life is ripe old age.
There were some who pleased God and were loved by him, and while living among sinners were taken up.
7–10 : The virtuous youth who dies early is better off than the ungodly who reach old age; cf. Isa 57.1–2.
They were caught up so that evil might not change their understanding or guile deceive their souls.
11 : , an allusion to Enoch, taken up by God (Gen 5.24, cf. Sir 44.16; Heb 11.5). 25.1 claims that the purpose of Enoch's removal was to preserve his character.
For the fascination of wickedness obscures what is good, and roving desire perverts the innocent mind.
Being perfected in a short time, they fulfilled long years;
for their souls were pleasing to the Lord, therefore he took them quickly from the midst of wickedness.
Yet the peoples saw and did not understand, or take such a thing to heart, that God's grace and mercy are with his elect, and that he watches over his holy ones.
The righteous who have died will condemn the ungodly who are living, and youth that is quickly perfected will condemn the prolonged old age of the unrighteous.
For they will see the end of the wise, and will not understand what the Lord purposed for them, and for what he kept them safe.
The unrighteous will see, and will have contempt for them, but the Lord will laugh them to scorn. After this they will become dishonored corpses, and an outrage among the dead forever;
because he will dash them speechless to the ground, and shake them from the foundations; they will be left utterly dry and barren, and they will suffer anguish, and the memory of them will perish.
16–19 : , an allusion to Daniel, a holy youth who confounds the wicked elders and saves Susanna (Sus 45–62).
18.b–19 : For similar threats see Isa 13.6–16; 24.17–23; 66.24; Jer 15.7–9.
They will come with dread when their sins are reckoned up, and their lawless deeds will convict them to their face.
1.1–6.21: Exhortation to love righteousness. This opening section embodies a complex argument for justice in an elegant concentric structure:
3.1–4.20: In defense of virtue and justice. In three diptychs the author contrasts the reality of immortal life for the righteous with the mere appearance of power in the life of the ungodly.
4.7–20: The third diptych: early death of a virtuous youth. The final diptych counters the first argument of the ungodly (2.1–9).
20 : , a parallel to the conclusion of the second diptych in which the children of the ungodly witness against their parents (4.6).
